facts: The patient's name is Michelle.
The patient injured her knee.
The injury occurred while skiing in Vermont.
The patient caught her ski while coming off the lift and fell.
The patient's knee popped during the fall.
The patient has had knee pain since the injury.
The patient was unable to bear weight after the injury.
The patient was taken down the hill by the ski patrol.
The patient's knee hurts on the outside.
The patient's knee feels like it won't hold her weight.
The patient's knee feels like it's going to buckle.
The patient was able to bend her knee immediately after the injury.
The patient is not able to bend her knee now.
The patient is able to straighten her knee somewhat.
The patient experiences pain when trying to straighten her knee all the way.
The patient does not have numbing or tingling in her toes.
The patient can feel her toes.
The patient is an active person.
The patient chases her two four-year-old children.
The patient has twins.
The patient's husband is able to help out at home.
The patient's husband works a job that takes him away from home quite a bit.
The patient is generally healthy.
The patient does not have high blood pressure.
The patient does not have diabetes.
The doctor performed a physical exam.
The patient's heart sounds are in a nice regular rate and rhythm.
The patient does not have any heart murmur.
The patient's lungs are clear to auscultation.
The patient injured her right knee.
The patient's right knee has edema.
The patient's right knee has an effusion.
The patient's right knee is painful to palpation.
The patient's right knee has decreased flexion and extension.
The patient's right knee has a positive Lachman sign.
The patient's right knee has a palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse.
The patient does not have leg edema in the ankle.
The patient had an x-ray of her right knee.
The x-ray did not show any bony abnormality.
The doctor suspects the patient may have ruptured her ACL.
The doctor ordered a knee MRI.
The patient is taking ibuprofen for pain.
Ibuprofen is helping the patient somewhat.
The patient does not want anything stronger for pain.
The doctor prescribed ibuprofen.
Ibuprofen is to be taken 600 to 800 milligrams every 8 hours as needed.
The doctor prescribed a brace for the patient's knee.
The patient wants to get back to running.
The patient is not currently active.
